greek_head: Ὦ Σώκρατες ἐδεόμην ἄττα σοι ἰδιολογήσασθαι εἰ σχολή κἂν εἰ ἀσχολία δὲ μὴ πάνυ τις μεγάλη ὅμως
greek_tokens: eimi swkraths deomai atta su idiologew ei sxolh kan ei asxolia de mh panu tis megas omws egw eneka poiew sxolh alla kai allos tugxanw sxolazw kai dh sew ge eneka kai panu alla ei tis boulomai legw ecesti boulomai oun deuro eis o o zeus o eleuqerios stoa ekpodwn apoxwrew ei sos dokew
english_head: Socrates, I was wanting to have some private talk with you, if you had time to spare; even if there is some demand, which is not particularly important, on your time, do spare some, nevertheless, for me. Why, in any case I happen to have time to spare, and for you, moreover, I have plenty. Well, you are free to say whatever you wish. Then do you mind if we step aside here from the street into the portico of Zeus the Liberator ? As you think best.

greek_head: Ἴωμεν δή Ὦ Σώκρατες πάντα τὰ φυτὰ κινδυνεύει τὸν αὐτὸν τρόπον ἔχειν καὶ τὰ ἐκ τῆς γῆς φυόμενα
greek_tokens: eimi dh eimi swkraths pas o futas kinduneuw o autos tropos exw kai o ek o gaia fuw kai o zwion o te allos kai anqrwpos kai gar en o futon radios egw outos gignomai osos o gaia gewrgew o paraskeuazw pas o pro o futeuw kai autos o futeuw epeidan de o futeuw biaw meta outos qerapeia o fus kai polus kai xalepos kai duskolos
english_head: Let us go, then. Socrates, it would seem that all growths follow the same course, both those that grow from the earth, and the animals, including man. In regard to the plants, as you know, we who cultivate the earth find it the easiest part of our work to make all our preparations that are needed before planting, and to do the planting itself; but when the plant begins to grow, thenceforward we have a great deal of difficult and vexatious business in tending the new growth.

greek_head: γίγνεται οὕτω δὲ ἔχειν ἔοικε καὶ τὸ περὶ τῶν ἀνθρώπων ἀπὸ τῶν ἐμαυτοῦ ἐγὼ πραγμάτων τεκμαίρομαι καὶ ἐς τἆλλα
greek_tokens: gignomai outws de exw eoika kai o peri o anqrwpos apo o emautou egw pragma tekmairomai kai eis allos kai gar egw o o uios outos eite futeia eite paidopoiia deomai autos onomazw pas radios gignomai o de trofh duskolos te kai aei en fobos peri autos deidw o men oun allos polus an eaw legw o de nun pareimi epiqumia outos panu egw fobew eimi men gar ou agennhs sfaleros de epiqumew gar dh outos egw eimi swkraths os
english_head: Such, it seems, is also the case in regard to men: I take my own concerns as evidence for judging of the rest. For indeed I have found the planting, or the procreation—whichever one ought to call it—of this son of mine the easiest thing in the world; but his upbringing has been vexatious and a constant source of alarm, so great are my fears for him. Among the many instances that I could mention, the desire which occupies him at the moment is a thing that especially alarms me: for it is not an ill-bred desire, but a dangerous one, since here we have him, Socrates, as he says, desiring to become wise.

greek_head: φησι σοφὸς γενέσθαι δοκῶ γάρ μοι τῶν ἡλικιωτῶν τινες αὐτοῦ καὶ δημοτῶν εἰς τὸ ἄστυ καταβαίνοντες λόγους τινὰς
greek_tokens: fhmi sofos gignomai dokazw gar egw o hlikiwths tis autos kai dhmoths eis o astu katabainw logos tis apomnhmoneuw diatarassw autos os zhlow kai pala egw pragma paraxew acia epimeleomai egw eautou kai xrhma telew tis o sofisths ostis autos sofos poihsis egw de o men xrhma kai elasswn
english_head: My opinion is that some of his fellow-townsmen, about his own age, who pay visits to the city, excite him with accounts of certain discussions they have heard there; and in his envy of these he has long been pestering me with the demand that I should take due thought for his needs, and pay fees to some sophist or other who will make him wise. Now I do not mind so much about the fees, but I believe he is running into no slight danger

greek_head: μέλει ἡγοῦμαι δὲ τοῦτον οὐκ εἰς μικρὸν κίνδυνον ἰέναι οἷ σπεύδει τέως μὲν οὖν αὐτὸν κατεῖχον παραμυθούμενος
greek_tokens: melos hgeomai de outos ou eis mikros kindunos eimi e speudw teos men oun autos katexw paramuqeomai epei de ouketi oios su eimi hgeomai kratistos eimi peiqw autos ina mh pollakis aneu egw suggignomai tis diafqeirw nun oun hkw epi autos outos ina tis outos o sofisths dokew eimi sunisthmi outos su oun egw eis kalos parafainw os an egw malista boulomai peri o toioutos mellw prassw sumbouleuw alla ei tis exis sumbouleuw ek os egw akouw ecesti te
english_head: where he is hastening. I did for a time restrain him with good advice; but since I am no longer able to do so, I believe my best course is to comply with his request, in order that he may not resort, perchance, behind my back to somebody who will corrupt him. So I have come now on this very business of placing this youth with one of these sophists, or purveyors of wisdom, as they are held to be. It is a happy chance, therefore, that has thrown you in our way, as I should be particularly glad, with this plan of action in my mind, to ask your advice. Come, if you have any advice to give

greek_head: καὶ χρή Ἀλλὰ μὲν δή ὦ Δημόδοκε καὶ λέγεταί γε συμβουλὴ
greek_tokens: kai xrh alla men dh eimi dhmodokos kai legw ge sumboulh ieron xrhma eimi eiper oun kai allos ostis eimi iera kai outos an eaw peri eis su nun sumbouleuw ou gar eimi peri ostis qeios an anqrwpos bouleuw h peri paideia kai eautou kai o eautou oikew proteros men oun egw te kai su sunomologew tis pote oiomai outos eimi peri e bouleuw mh gar pollakis egw
english_head: on what you have heard from me, you not only may, but should, give it. Well, you know, Demodocus, they do say that advice is a holy thing. And so, if ever it is to be accounted holy, it must be in this instance, in which you now seek it. For there is no more divine matter on which a mortal could take counsel than the education either

greek_head: μὲν ἄλλο τι αὐτὸ ὑπολαμβάνω σὺ δὲ ἄλλο κἄπειτα πόρρω που τῆς συνουσίας αἰσθώμεθα γελοῖοι ὄντες ἐγώ τε ὁ συμβουλεύων
greek_tokens: men allos tis autos upolambanw su de allos epeita prosw pou o sunousia aisqanomai geloios eimi egw te o sumbouleuw kai su o sumbouleuw mhdeis o autos hgeomai alla egw dokew orqos legw eimi swkraths kai poiew xrh outws kai legos ge orqos ou mentoi pantapasi ge mikros gar tis metatiqhmi ennoew gar mh kai o meirakiskos outos ou outos epiqumew e egw autos oiomai
english_head: of himself or of his relations. Now, first of all, let you and me come to an agreement as to what we suppose that this thing can be, on which we are taking counsel; for it may happen that I conceive it to be one thing, and you another, and then when we have proceeded some little way in our conference, we may perceive how ridiculous we are, I the adviser and you the advised, in having no common ground in our notions. Why, I think you are right there, Socrates, and we should do as you suggest. Yes, I am right, but yet not entirely, because I have a slight change to make. For it occurs to me that

greek_head: ἐπιθυμεῖν ἀλλ' ἑτέρου εἶτ' αὖ ἡμεῖς ἔτι ἀτοπώτεροι ὦμεν περὶ ἄλλου του βουλευόμενοι ὀρθότατον οὖν μοι δοκεῖ
greek_tokens: epiqumew alla eteros eimi au egw eti atopos eimi peri allos tis bouleuw orqos oun egw dokew eimi apo autos outos arxw diapunqanomai ostis kai eimi e epiqumew kinduneuw goun outws beltistos eimi ws su legw eipon dh egw tis kalos onoma o neaniskos tis autos prosagoreuw qeaghs onoma outos eimi swkraths kalos ge eimi dhmodokos o uios o onoma eqw kai
english_head: this youngster may not be desiring the thing that we suppose him to desire, but something else, and there again we may be still more absurdly taking counsel on some other thing. Hence our most proper course, it seems to me, is to begin with the youth himself, and inquire of him what it actually is that he desires. It does rather look, in fact, as though our best way would be thus, as you suggest. Then tell me, what is the young person’s goodly name: how are we to address him? Theages is his name, Socrates.

greek_head: ἱεροπρεπές εἰπὲ δὴ ἡμῖν ὦ Θέαγες ἐπιθυμεῖν φῂς σοφὸς γενέσθαι καὶ ἀξιοῖς σου τὸν πατέρα τόνδε ἐξευρεῖν ἀνδρός
greek_tokens: ieroprephs eipon dh egw eimi qeages epiqumew fhmi sofos gignomai kai aciow su o pathr ode eceuriskw anhr tis sunousia toioutos ostis su sofos poihsis nai sofos de kalew poteros o episthmwn peri ostis an episthmwn eimi h o mh o episthmwn egw tis oun ou didaskw su o pathr kai paideuw o enqade e allos paideuw e o kalh agaqos pathr uios oios gramma te kai kiqarizw kai palaiw kai o allos agwnia egw ge
english_head: Goodly is the name, Demodocus, and holy-sounding, that you have bestowed on your son. Tell me, then, Theages, do you say you desire to become wise, and do you require your father here to find out a school of some man who is qualified to make you wise? Yes. And which sort of man do you call wise, those who have knowledge of such and such a thing, whatever it may be, or those who have not? Those who have knowledge, I say. Well now, has not your father taught and educated you in the subjects which form the education of everyone else here—all the sons of noble and honorable fathers—in letters, I mean, and harping and wrestling and the other sorts of contest?

greek_head: Ἔτι οὖν οἴει τινὸς ἐπιστήμης ἐλλείπειν ἧς προσήκει ὑπὲρ σοῦ τὸν πατέρα ἐπιμεληθῆναι
greek_tokens: eti oun oiomai tis episthmh elleipw eis proshkw uper sew o pathr epimeleomai egw tis eimi outos eipon kai egw ina su xarizomai oida kai outos eimi swkraths epei pollakis egw autos erw alla outos ecepithdes pros sos legw ws dh ou eidoi e egw epiqumew toioutos gar eteros kai pros egw maxomai te kai ou eqelw egw oudeis sunisthmi alla o men emprosqen su eimi pros outos erw
english_head: Yes, he has. And you think you are still lacking in some knowledge which it behoves your father to provide for you? I do What knowledge is it? Tell us on our side, that we may oblige you. He knows it, as well as I, Socrates, since I have often told him; only he says this to you of set purpose, making as if he did not know what I desire. For he assails me too with other statements of the same sort, and refuses to place me with any instructor.

greek_head: ὥσπερ ἄνευ μαρτύρων λεγόμενα νυνὶ δὲ ἐμὲ ποίησαι μάρτυρα καὶ ἐναντίον ἐμοῦ κάτειπε τίς ἐστιν αὕτη ἡ σοφία ἧς
greek_tokens: wsper aneu marturos legw nuni de egw poiew martus kai enantion egw kateipon tis eimi outos o sofia eis epiqumew ferw gar ei epiqumew outos h e anqrwpos o ploion kubernaw kai egw su tugxanw anerwtaw eimi qeages tis endehs eimi sofia memfomai o pathr ostis ou eqelw su sunisthmi para os an su sofos gignomai tis an egw apokrinw tis autos eimi ara ou kubernhtikos nai
english_head: Well, what you said to him before was spoken, as it were, without witnesses; but now you shall take me as a witness, and declare before me what is this wisdom that you desire. Come now; suppose you desired the wisdom whereby men steer a ship, and I happened to put this further question to you: Theages, what wisdom is it that you lack, when you blame your father for refusing to place you with people who would enable you to become wise? What answer would you have given me? What wisdom would you name? The steersman’s art, would you not? Yes.

greek_head: Εἰ δὲ ἐπιθυμῶν ταύτην τὴν σοφίαν εἶναι σοφὸς ᾗ τὰ ἅρματα κυβερνῶσιν εἶτ' ἐμέμφου τῷ πατρί ἐμοῦ αὖ
greek_tokens: ei de epiqumew outos o sofia eimi sofos h o arma kubernaw eimi memfomai o pathr egw au erwtaw tis eimi outos o sofia tis an apokrinw autos eimi ara ou hnioxikos nai eis de dh nun tugxanw epiqumew poteros anwnumos tis eimi h exis onoma oimh egw exw poteros oun autos men oida ou mentoi o ge onoma h kai o onoma kai o onoma egw tis oun eimi eipon
english_head: And if a desire to be wise in the wisdom whereby they steer chariots led you to blame your father, and I asked what wisdom this was, what would you name in reply? The charioteer’s art, would you not? Yes. And is that which you happen to be desiring now a nameless one, or has it a name? I should say it has a name. Now do you know it, though not its name, or do you know its name as well? I know its name as well. Then what is it? Tell me.

greek_head: Τί δὲ ἄλλο ὦ Σώκρατες αὐτῇ ὄνομά τις φαίη ἂν εἶναι ἀλλ' ἢ σοφίαν
greek_tokens: tis de allos eimi swkraths autos onoma tis fhmi an eimi alla h sofia oukoun kai o hnioxeia sofia eimi h amaqia dokew su eimi ou egw alla sofia nai h tis xraomai ou h ippos efisthmi zeugos arxw nai oukoun kai o kubernhtikos sofia eimi egw dokew ara ou outos h ploion efisthmi arxw outos men oun eis de dh su epiqumew o sofia tis eimi h tis
english_head: What other name, Socrates, can one give it but wisdom? And the driver’s art too is wisdom? Or do you think it is ignorance? I do not. You call it wisdom? Yes. What use do we make of it? Is it not the art whereby we know how to govern a team of horses? Yes. And the steersman’s art too is wisdom? I think so. Is not this the art whereby we know how to govern ships? Yes, it is. And the wisdom that you so desire, what is it?

greek_head: ἐπιστάμεθα ἄρχειν Ἐμοὶ μὲν δοκεῖ ᾗ τῶν ἀνθρώπων
greek_tokens: efisthmi arxw egw men dokew h o anqrwpos mhs h o kamnw ou dhta iatrikos gar outos eimi eimi gar nai alla h o aeidw efisthmi en o xoros arxw ou mousikh gar outos ge panu ge alla h o gumnazw efisthmi arxw ou gumnastikos gar outos ge nai alla h o tis poiew proqumeomai eipon wsper egw sos o emprosqen
english_head: That whereby we know how to govern whom? To govern men, I imagine. Sick men, do you mean? Oh, no. For that is medicine, is it not? Yes. Well, that whereby we know how to govern the singers in a chorus? No. For that is music? To be sure. Well, that whereby we know how to govern men in gymnastic training? No. For that is gymnastics? Yes. Well, to govern people who do what? Endeavor your best to speak, as I did to you at the beginning.

greek_head: Ἧι τῶν ἐν τῇ πόλει ἔμοιγε δοκεῖ Οὐκοῦν ἐν τῇ πόλει εἰσὶν καὶ οἱ κάμνοντες
greek_tokens: h o en o polis egw dokew oukoun en o polis eimi kai e kamnw nai alla ou outos legos monos alla kai o allos o en o polis ara ge manqanw os legw texnh dokew gar egw legw ou h o qerizw efisthmi arxw kai trugaw kai o futeuw kai speirw kai alow outos men gar gewrgikos h outos arxw eimi gar nai
english_head: To govern the people in the city, I imagine. And are the sick people also in the city? Yes, but I mean not these only, but all the rest who are in the city besides. Do I understand what art it is that you mean? For you strike me as meaning, not that whereby we know how to govern reapers and harvesters and planters and sowers and threshers, for it is the farmer’s art whereby we govern these, is it not? Yes.

greek_head: Οὐδέ γε οἶμαι ᾗ τῶν πριζόντων καὶ τρυπώντων καὶ ξεόντων καὶ τορνευόντων συμπάντων ἐπιστάμεθα ἄρχειν
greek_tokens: oude ge oimh h o priw kai trupaw kai cew kai torneuw sumpas efisthmi arxw ou outos legw outos men gar ou tektonikos nai alla isos h outos te pas kai autos o gewrgew kai o tektwn kai o dhmiourgew apas kai o idiwths kai o gunaikow kai androw outos isos legw o sofia outos pala eimi swkraths boulomai legw
english_head: Nor, I suppose, do you mean that whereby we know how to govern sawyers and borers and planers and turners, as a class together; for is not that carpentry? Yes. But perhaps it is that whereby we govern, not only all these, but farmers themselves also, and carpenters, and all craftsmen and ordinary people, whether men or women: that, perhaps, is the wisdom you mean. That, Socrates, is what I have been intending to mean all the time.

greek_head: Ἔχεις οὖν εἰπεῖν Αἴγισθος ὁ Ἀγαμέμνονα ἀποκτείνας ἐν Ἄργει ἆρα τούτων ἦρχεν ὧν σὺ λέγεις τῶν τε
greek_tokens: exis oun eipon aigisqos o agamemnwn apokteinw en argew ara outos arxw os su legw o te dhmiourgew kai idiwths kai androw kai gunaikow sumpas h allos tis ou alla outos tis de dh phleus o aiakos en fqia ou o autos outos arxw nai periandros de o kuyelos arxw en korinqos hdh akouw gignomai egw ou o autos outos arxw en o eautou polis
english_head: Then can you tell me whether Aegisthus, who slew Agamemnon in Argos, governed all these people that you mean craftsmen and ordinary people, both men and women, or some other persons? No, just those. Well now, did not Peleus, son of Aeacus, govern these same people in Phthia? Yes. And have you ever heard of Periander, son of Cypselus, and how he governed at Corinth? I have. Did he not govern these same people in his city?

greek_head: Ναί Τί δὲ Ἀρχέλαον τὸν Περδίκκου τὸν νεωστὶ τοῦτον
greek_tokens: nai tis de arxelaos o perdikkos o newsti outos arxw en makedonia ou o autos hgeomai outos arxw egw ippios de o peisistratos en ode o polis arxw tinw oiomai arxw ou outos pws gar ou eipon an oun egw tis epwnumios exis bakis te kai sibulla kai o hmedapos amfilutos tis gar allos eimi swkraths plhn ge xrhsmwdos
english_head: Yes. Or again, do you not consider that Archelaus, son of Perdiccas, who governed recently in Macedonia, governed these same people? I do. And who do you think were governed by Hippias, son of Peisistratus, who governed in this city? Were they not these people ? To be sure they were. Now, can you tell me what appellation is given to Bacis and Sibyl and our native Amphilytus? Why, soothsayers, of course, Socrates.

greek_head: Ὀρθῶς λέγεις ἀλλὰ καὶ τούσδε μοι οὕτω πειρῶ ἀποκρίνασθαι τίνα ἐπωνυμίαν ἔχει Ἱππίας καὶ Περίανδρος
greek_tokens: orqos legw alla kai ode egw outws peiraw apokrinw tis epwnumios exis ippios kai periandros dia o autos arxh oimh men turannos tis gar allos oukoun ostis epiqumew o anqrwpos o en o polis sumpas arxw o autos arxh outos epiqumew turannikos kai turannos eimi fainw oukoun outos epiqumew su fhmi eoika ge ek os egw eipon eimi miaros turanneuw ara egw epiqumew pala
english_head: That is correct. But try to answer me in that way regarding those others—Hippias and Periander: what appellation is given them on account of their government? Despots, I suppose; it must be that. And when a man desires to govern the whole of the people in his city, he desires the same government as those did—despotism, and to be a despot? Apparently. And it is this that you say you desire? It seems so, from what I have said.

greek_head: ἐμέμφου τῷ πατρὶ ὅτι σε οὐκ ἔπεμπεν εἰς διδασκάλου τυραννοδιδασκάλου τινός καὶ σύ ὦ Δημόδοκε οὐκ αἰσχύνῃ
greek_tokens: memfomai o pathr ostis su ou pempw eis didaskalos turannodidaskalos tis kai su eimi dhmodokos ou aisxunh pala eidoi e epiqumew outos kai exw oqi pempw autos dhmiourgos an poiew o sofia eis epiqumew epeita fqonew te autos kai ou eqelw pempw alla nun oraw epei enantion egw katerw su koinh bouleuw egw te kai su eis tis an autos pempw kai dia o tis sunousia sofos an gignomai turannos
english_head: You scoundrel! So you were desiring to govern us, all the time that you were blaming your father for not sending you to some seminary of despots! And you, Demodocus, are you not ashamed of having known all the time what he is desiring, and though you could have sent him where you would have made him an expert in the wisdom which he desires, actually grudging it to him and refusing to send him? But now, look here, as he has declared against you in my presence, shall you and I consult together on the question of whose school we shall send him to, and whose classes will help him to become a wise despot?

greek_head: Ναὶ μὰ Δία ὦ Σώκρατες βουλευώμεθα δῆτα ὡς δοκεῖ γέ μοι βουλῆς δεῖν περὶ τούτου οὐ φαύλης
greek_tokens: nai ma dios eimi swkraths bouleuw dhta ws dokew ge egw bouleus dew peri outos ou faulos eaw agaqos diapunqanomai autos proteros ikanos punqanomai dh tis oun an ei euripidhs tis prosxraomai eimi qeages euripidhs gar pou fhmi sofos turannos o sofos sunousia ei oun eromai tis o euripidhs eimi euripidhs o tis
english_head: Yes, in faith, Socrates, let us certainly consult, as I feel this is a matter on which no slight counsel is needed. By and by, my good sir. Let us first cross-examine him thoroughly. Examine him then. Well now, what if we called in Euripides to our aid, Theages? For you know Euripides says:Despots are wise by converse with the wise. Soph. Fr. 14.1 Now, if someone should ask Euripides: Euripides, in what

greek_head: σοφῶν συνουσίᾳ φῂς σοφοὺς εἶναι τοὺς τυράννους ὥσπερ ἂν εἰ εἰπόντα
greek_tokens: sofos sunousia fhmi sofos eimi o turannos wsper an ei eipon sofos gewrgos o sofos sunousia arow o tis sofos tis an egw apokrinw ara an allos tis h o o gewrgikos ou alla outos tis de ei eipon sofos mageiros o sofos sunousia ei arow o tis sofos tis an egw apokrinw ou ostis o mageiros nai tis de ei sofos palaisth o sofos sunousia eipon ei arow o tis sofos ara ou an o
english_head: are these men wise, by whose converse you say that despots are wise? I mean, suppose he had said:Farmers are wise by converse with the wise,and we had asked him,—Wise in what?—what answer would he have given us? Surely none other than,—In farming. That, and none other. Or again, if he had said:Piemen are wise by converse with the wise,and we had asked him, Wise in what?—what answer would he have given us? He would have said,—In the pie-making business,—would he not? Yes. Or again, if he had saidWrestlers are wise by converse with the wise,and we had asked him, Wise in what?—would he not reply,—

greek_head: παλαίειν ἔφη Ναί
greek_tokens: palaiw fhmi nai epei de eipon sofos turannos o sofos sunousia egw erwtaw o tis sofos legw eimi euripidhs tis an fhmi poios an eimi outos alla ma diw ou oida egw alla boulomai egw su eipon ei su boulomai outos eimi o fhmi anakrewn o kallikrithn efisthmi h ou oida o aeisma egw tis oun toioutos tis kai su sunousia epiqumew
english_head: In wrestling? Yes. But as he said:Despots are wise by converse with the wise,? Soph. Fr. 14.1 and we ask him,—In what do you mean that the latter are wise, Euripides?—what will he reply? What sort of subjects will he mention here? Why, upon my word, I for my part do not know. Well, do you mind if I tell you? If you do not mind. They are the same subjects that Anacreon said Callicrite understood; or do you not know the ode? I do. Well then, do you desire to partake in some instruction of that sort from any man

greek_head: ἀνδρὸς ὅστις τυγχάνει ὁμότεχνος ὢν Καλλικρίτῃ τῇ Κυάνης καὶ ἐπίσταται τυραννικά ὥσπερ ἐκείνην ἔφη ὁ ποιητής
greek_tokens: anhr ostis tugxanw omotexnos eimi kallikrith o kuanos kai efisthmi turannikos wsper ekeinos fhmi o poihths ina kai su egw turannos gignomai kai o polis pala eimi swkraths skwptw kai paizw pros egw tis de ou outos fhmi o sofia epiqumew h pas an o poliths arxw outos de poa allos tis h turannos an eaw euxomai men an oimh egw turannos gignomai
english_head: who is a fellow-craftsman of Callicrite, daughter of Cyane, and knows all about despotism as she did, according to the poet, in order that you may become a despot over us and our city? You are joking all this time, Socrates, and making fun of me. Why, do you not say that you desire that wisdom which will enable you to govern all the citizens? And in doing that, will you be anything else but a despot ? I should indeed pray, I imagine, that I might become a despot,

greek_head: μάλιστα μὲν πάντων ἀνθρώπων εἰ δὲ μή ὡς πλείστων καὶ σύ γ' ἂν οἶμαι καὶ οἱ ἄλλοι πάντες ἄνθρωποι ἔτι δέ γε
greek_tokens: malista men pas anqrwpos ei de mh ws pleistos kai su ge an oimh kai e allos pas anqrwpos eti de ge isos mallon qeos gignomai alla ou outos elegos epiqumew alla tis dh eimi pote e epiqumew ou o poliths fhmi arxw epiqumew ou bia ge oude wsper e turannos alla ekwn wsper kai e allos e en o polis ellogimos anhr ara ge legw wsper qemistoklehs kai periklaw kai kimwn kai osos o politikos deinos gignomai nh dios outos legos tis oun ei o ippikos tugxanw epiqumew sofos
english_head: if possible, over all men, and failing that, over as many as might be; so would you, I imagine, and everybody else besides: nay, even more, I daresay, that I might become a god; but I did not say I desired that. Well, what on earth then is it that you do desire? Do you not say you desire to govern the citizens? Yes, but not by force, or as despots do, but with their consent, as is done by all the other men of importance in the state. Do you mean, as by Themistocles and Pericles and Cimon, and by all those who have shown themselves able statesmen? Yes, in good earnest, I mean those people.

greek_head: γενέσθαι παρὰ τίνας ἂν ἀφικόμενος ᾠήθης δεινὸς ἔσεσθαι ἱππεύς ἦ παρ' ἄλλους τινὰς ἢ τοὺς ἱππικούς
greek_tokens: gignomai para tis an afikneomai oiomai deinos eimi ippeus eimi para allos tis h o ippikos ma dios ou egw alla para autos au o deinos eimi outos kai os eimi te ippos kai xraomai ekastote kai oikew kai allotrios polus dhlos ostis tis de ei o akontistikos sofos boulomai gignomai ou para o akontistikos oiomai an erxomai sofos eimi outos os eimi te akontion kai polus kai allotrios kai
english_head: Then what if you chanced to desire to become wise in horsemanship? To whom would you have had to resort before expecting to be a clever horseman? To whom else but the horse-masters? To none else, I am sure. And moreover, you would go to the actual men who are clever at the business, and who have horses and constantly use them in great numbers, both their own and other people’s? Obviously I should. And what if you wished to become wise in javelin-throwing? Would you not expect to get this wisdom by having resorted to those javelin-masters who have javelins and who constantly use javelins,

greek_head: οἰκείοις ἑκάστοτε χρῶνται ἀκοντίοις Ἔμοιγε δοκεῖ
greek_tokens: oikew ekastote xraomai akontion egw dokew legos dh egw epei de dh o politikos boulomai sofos gignomai oiomai para allos tis afikneomai sofos eimi h o politikos outos o autos te deinos eimi o politikos kai xraomai ekastote o te eautou polis kai allos polus kai ellhnis prosomilew polis kai barbaros h dokew allos tis suggignomai sofos eimi outos o outos alla ou autos outos
english_head: both other people’s and their own, in great numbers? I think so. Then pray tell me, since it is your wish to become wise in state-matters, do you expect to get your wisdom by resorting to any other persons than those statesmen, who not only have their own ability in state-matters, but have constant dealings with other cities besides their own, by their intercourse alike with Greek cities and with foreign peoples? Or do you think to get wisdom in their business by resorting to any other persons than these particular men?

greek_head: Ἀκήκοα γάρ ὦ Σώκρατες οὕς σέ φασιν λέγειν τοὺς λόγους ὅτι τούτων τῶν πολιτικῶν ἀνδρῶν οἱ ὑεῖς οὐδὲν
greek_tokens: akouw gar eimi swkraths os sos fhmi legw o logos ostis outos o politikos androw e uios oudeis beltiwn eimi h e o skutotomos kai egw dokew alhqhs legw ek os egw dunamai aisqanomai anohtos an oun eimi ei oiomai tis outos egw men an paradidwmi o eautou sofia o de uios o eautou mhdeis wfelew ei tis oios su eimi eis outos wfelew allos ostis anqrwpos tis oun an eimi beltistos androw xraw sautou ei su epei gignomai us toioutos pragma parexw kai fhmi
english_head: Well, Socrates, I have heard of the argument that you are said to put forward—that the sons of those statesmen are no better men than the sons of shoemakers ; and in my opinion your words are very true, from what I am able to gather. Hence I should be an utter fool if I supposed that any of these men would impart his wisdom to me when he never was of any use to his own son, as he would have been, if he were able to be of use in this matter to anyone at all in the world. Then which way, most excellent sir, would you turn if, when you came to have a son, he should trouble you in the same manner,

greek_head: μὲν ἂν ἐπιθυμεῖν ἀγαθὸς γενέσθαι ζωγράφος καὶ μέμφοιτο σοὶ τῷ πατρὶ ὅτι οὐκ ἐθέλεις ἀναλίσκειν εἰς αὐτὸν τούτων
greek_tokens: men an epiqumew agaqos gignomai zwgrafos kai memfomai sos o pathr ostis ou eqelw analiskw eis autos outos autos eneka argurion o de dhmiourgos autos outos o zwgrafos atimazw te kai mh boulomai para autos manqanw h o aulhths boulomai aulhths gignomai h o kiqarisths exw an autos ostis xraomai kai opoi pempw allote mh eqelw para outos manqanw ma dios ou egw
english_head: and tell you he desired to become a good painter, and should blame you, his father, for refusing to spend money on him for that very purpose, but at the same time should disregard the practitioners of that very thing, the painters, and decline to learn from them? Or the flute-players, when he wished to become a flute-player, or the harp-players? Would you know what to do with him, and where else you should send him if he refused to learn from these? Upon my word, I should not.

greek_head: Νῦν οὖν ταὐτὰ ταῦτα αὐτὸς πρὸς τὸν πατέρα ποιῶν θαυμάζεις καὶ μέμφῃ εἰ ἀπορεῖ ὅτι σοι χρήσηται καὶ ὅποι
greek_tokens: nun oun tautos outos autos pros o pathr poa qaumazw kai memfomai ei aforaw ostis su xraw kai opoi pempw epei aqhnaia ge o kalh agaqos o politikos ostis an boulomai sunisthmi su os su proic suneimi kai ama men argurion ou analwsis ama de polus mallon eudokimhsis para o polus anqrwpos h allos tis suneimi tis oun eimi swkraths ou kai su o kalh agaqos ei androw ei gar su egw eqelw suneimi ecarkew kai oudeis allos zhtew
english_head: And do you now, when you are behaving in just the same way to your father, feel surprised and blame him for being at a loss what to do with you and where to send you? Why, we are ready to place you with any well-bred Athenian statesman you may choose, who will train you free of charge ; and so not only will you be at no expense of money, but will gain far greater commendation amongst the mass of men than if you studied with anyone else. But then, Socrates, are not you too one of our well-bred gentlemen? Indeed, if you will agree to instruct me, I am content and seek no other.

greek_head: Τί τοῦτο λέγεις Θέαγες Ὦ Σώκρατες οὐ μέντοι κακῶς λέγει καὶ ἅμα μὲν
greek_tokens: tis outos legw qeages eimi swkraths ou mentoi kakos legw kai ama men egw xarizomai ws egw ou esqw ostis outos megas an ermaion hgeomai h ei outos te areskw o sew sunousia kai su eqelw outos suneimi kai mentoi kai aisxunw legw ws sfodra boulomai alla egw amfoteros su deomai sos su eqelw outos suneimi kai sos mh zhtew allos mhdeis suggignomai h swkratew kai egw polus kai foberos apallassw
english_head: What do you mean by that, Theages? Nay, Socrates, there is nothing amiss in what he says, and you will oblige me at the same time; for I should count it the greatest possible stroke of luck if he should welcome your instruction and you also should consent to instruct him. Nay, indeed, I am quite ashamed to say how keenly I wish it; but I entreat you both—you, to consent to teach Theages, and you, to seek the teaching of no one else than Socrates; you will thus relieve me

greek_head: φροντίδων ὡς νῦν πάνυ φοβοῦμαι ὑπὲρ τούτου μή τινι ἄλλῳ ἐντύχῃ οἵῳ τοῦτον διαφθεῖραι
greek_tokens: frontis ws nun panu fobew uper outos mh tis allos entugxanw oios outos diafqeirw mhketi nun eimi pathr uper ge egw fobew eiper oios su ei peiqw outos o emos sunousia prosdexomai panu kalos legw eimi swkraths pros sos de an hdh eaw o meta outos logos egw gar su etoimos eimi ws dia braxea eipon kai egw kai o emos ws oios te oikeios parexw ostis an deomai embraxu ean qeaghs outos
english_head: of a harassing load of anxiety. For just now I am sorely afraid of his falling in with some other person who is likely to corrupt him. Have no more fears for me now, father, so long as you are able to persuade him to receive me as his pupil. Very rightly spoken. Socrates, from now onward we must address ourselves to you; for I am ready, in short, to place both myself and all that I hold dearest of what is mine in your hands—whatever you may require,

greek_head: ἀσπάζῃ τε καὶ εὐεργετῇς ὅτι ἂν οἷός τε ᾖς Ὦ Δημόδοκε τὸ μὲν ἐσπουδακέναι σε οὐ θαυμάζω
greek_tokens: aspazomai te kai euergetew ostis an oios te eimi eimi dhmodokos o men spoudazw su ou qaumazw eiper oiomai upo egw malista an su outos wfelew ou gar oida uper ostis an tis new exw mallon spoudazw h uper uios eautou opws ws beltistos eimi opoqen de dokew su outos ws egw an mallon o sos uios oios su eimi wfelew pros o poliths agaqos gignomai h su autos kai opoqen outos oiomai egw mallon h sos autos wfelew outos
english_head: absolutely—if you will open your arms to Theages here, and do him any service that you can. Demodocus, your zeal is no wonder to me, if you suppose that I especially could be of use to him; for I know of nothing for which a sensible man could be more zealous than for his own son’s utmost improvement. But how you came to form this opinion, that I would be better able to be of use to your son in his aim of becoming a good citizen than you would yourself, and how he came to suppose that I rather than yourself would be of use to him—this does fill me with wonder. For you,

greek_head: πάνυ θαυμάζω σὺ γὰρ πρῶτον μὲν πρεσβύτερος εἶ ἐμοῦ ἔπειτα πολλὰς ἤδη ἀρχὰς καὶ τὰς μεγίστας Ἀθηναίοις ἦρξας
greek_tokens: panu qaumazw su gar proteros men presbus ei egw epeita polus hdh arxh kai o megas aqhnaia arxw kai timaw upo anagurasios te o dhmoths polus malista kai upo o allos polis oudeis hsswn egw de outos oudeis enoraw oudeteros su epeita ei ara o men o politikos androw sunousia qeaghs ode katafronew allos de tis zhtew e paideuw epaggellw oios te eimi neos anqrwpos eimi entauqa kai prodikos o kews kai gorgihs
english_head: in the first place, are my elder, and further, you have held in your time many of the highest offices in Athens, and are respected by the people of Anagyrus above all your fellow-townsmen, and by the whole state as much as any man, whereas neither of you can notice anything like this about me. And moreover, if Theages here does despise the instruction of our statesmen, and is looking for some other persons who profess to be able to educate young people, we have here Prodicus of Ceos, Gorgias of Leontini, Polus of Acragas,

greek_head: ὁ Λεοντῖνος καὶ Πῶλος ὁ Ἀκραγαντῖνος καὶ ἄλλοι πολλοί οἳ οὕτω σοφοί εἰσιν ὥστε εἰς τὰς πόλεις ἰόντες πείθουσι
greek_tokens: o leontinos kai pwlos o akragantinos kai allos polus e outws sofos eimi wste eis o polis eimi peiqw o neos o gennaios te kai plousios os ecesti o poliths os an boulomai proic suneimi outos peiqw apoleipw o ekeinos sunousia autos suneimi proskatatiqhmi argurion panu polus misqos kai xaris pros outos oida outos tis eoika eimi proairew kai o uios su kai autos sos egw de ou
english_head: and many more, who are so wise that they go to our cities and persuade the noblest and wealthiest of our young men—who have the choice of learning from any citizen they choose, free of charge—they persuade them to abandon that instruction and learn from them, with a deposit, besides, of a large sum of money as their fee, and to feel thankful in addition. Some of these persons might naturally have been chosen both by your son and by yourself, in preference to me;

greek_head: εἰκός οὐδὲν γὰρ τούτων ἐπίσταμαι τῶν μακαρίων τε καὶ καλῶν μαθημάτων ἐπεὶ ἐβουλόμην ἄν ἀλλὰ καὶ λέγω
greek_tokens: eoika oudeis gar outos efisthmi o makarios te kai kalh maqhma epei boulomai an alla kai legos dhpou aei ostis egw tugxanw ws epos eipon oudeis efisthmi plhn ge mikros tis maqhma o erwtikos outos mentoi o maqhma para ostis poiew deinos eimi kai o progignomai anqrwpos kai o nun oraw eimi pathr o swkratew ou panu egw dokew tis eti eqelw egw sundiatribw epei o ge emos etoimos
english_head: for I have no knowledge of those fair and beatific subjects of study: I only wish that I had. But what I always say, you know, is that I am in the position of knowing practically nothing except one little subject, that of love-matters. In this subject, however, I claim to be skilled above anybody who has ever lived or is now living in the world. Do you see, father? Socrates does not seem to me to be at all willing now to spend his time on me; for there is readiness enough on my part,

greek_head: ἐὰν οὗτος ἐθέλῃ ἀλλὰ ταῦτα παίζων πρὸς ἡμᾶς λέγει ἐπεὶ ἐγὼ οἶδα τῶν ἐμῶν ἡλικιωτῶν καὶ ὀλίγῳ πρεσβυτέρων
greek_tokens: ean outos eqelw alla outos paizw pros egw legw epei egw oida o emew hlikiwths kai oligos presbus e prin men outos suneimi oudeis acios eimi epei de suggignomai outos en panu oligos xronos pas beltiwn fainw os proteros xeirwn oida oun oios outos eimi eimi pais dhmodokos nai ma dios egw ostis ean su boulomai kai egw oios su eimi toioutos gignomai oios kai ekeinos
english_head: if he is willing. But he is only jesting in what he has just told us. For I know of some of my equals in age, and some a little older, who were of no account before they learnt from him, but after beginning to learn from him have in a very short time proved themselves superior to all whose inferiors they were before. And do you know what the meaning of it is, son of Demodocus? Yes, on my soul, I do—that, if it be your pleasure, I too shall be able to become such as those others are.

greek_head: Οὔκ ὠγαθέ ἀλλά σε λέληθεν οἷον τοῦτ' ἔστιν ἐγὼ δέ σοι φράσω ἔστι γάρ τι θείᾳ μοίρᾳ παρεπόμενον ἐμοὶ
greek_tokens: ou agaqos alla su lanqanw oios outos eimi egw de su frazw eimi gar tis qeia moira parepomai egw ek pais arxw daimonion eimi de outos fwnh o otan gignomai aei egw shmainw o an mellw prassw outos apotroph protrepw de oudepote kai ean tis egw o filos anakoinow kai gignomai o fwnh tautos outos apotrepw kai ou eaw prassw kai outos su martus parexw xarmidhs gar outos gignwskw o kalos
english_head: No, good sir, the meaning of it escapes you; but I will tell it you. There is something spiritual which, by a divine dispensation, has accompanied me from my childhood up. It is a voice that, when it occurs, always indicates to me a prohibition of something I may be about to do, but never urges me on to anything; and if one of my friends consults me and the voice occurs, the same thing happens: it prohibits, and does not allow him to act. And I will produce witnesses to convince you of these facts. You know our Charmides here, who has grown so handsome, the son of Glaucon:

greek_head: γενόμενον τὸν Γλαύκωνος οὗτός ποτε ἐτύγχανε ἐμοὶ ἀνακοινούμενος μέλλων ἀσκήσειν στάδιον εἰς Νεμέαν καὶ εὐθὺς
greek_tokens: gignomai o glaukwn outos pote tugxanw egw anakoinew mellw askew stadion eis nemeios kai euqus autos arxw legw ostis mellw askew gignomai o fwnh kai egw diakwluw te autos kai eipon ostis legw su metacu gignomai egw o fwnh o o daimonion alla mh askew isos fhmi shmainw su ostis ou nikaw egw de kan mh mellw nikh gumnazw ge outos o xronos wfelew outos eipon askew acios oun punqanomai autos
english_head: he once happened to be consulting me on his intention of training for the Nemean races, and he had no sooner begun to say that he intended to train than the voice occurred, and I tried to prevent him, saying—Just as you were speaking my spirit-voice has occurred: no, you must not train. Perhaps, said he, it indicates to you that I shall not win; but even if I am not to win, at any rate the exercise I shall get in the meantime will do me good. So saying, he went and trained; and so you may as well inquire of him

greek_head: ἃ αὐτῷ συνέβη ἀπὸ ταύτης τῆς ἀσκήσεως εἰ δὲ βούλεσθε τὸν Τιμάρχου ἀδελφὸν Κλειτόμαχον ἔρεσθε τί εἶπεν αὐτῷ
greek_tokens: o autos sumbainw apo outos o askhsis ei de boulomai o timarxos adelfos kleitomaxos eromai tis eipon autos timarxos hnika apoqnhskw eimi euqus o daimonion ekeinos te kai euaqlos o stadiodromhs os timarxos upodexomai feugw eraomai gar su ostis eipon autos outos tis eimi kleitomaxos fhmi egw mentoi erxomai apoqnhskw nuni dioti swkratew ou eqelw peiqw tis dh oun pote outos eipon o timarxos egw frazw oste
english_head: as to the results he got from his training. Or if you like, ask Cleitomachus, brother of Timarchus, what Timarchus said to him when he was going straight to the prison to meet his death, he and Euathlus the racing runner, who had harbored Timarchus as a fugitive; for he will tell you that the words he spoke to him were these: What? Cleitomachus, he said, I tell you I am going to my death now, because I would not take Socrates’ advice. Now, why on earth did Timarchus say that? I will tell you. When Timarchus and Philemon,

greek_head: ἀνίστατο ἐκ τοῦ συμποσίου ὁ Τίμαρχος καὶ Φιλήμων ὁ Φιλημονίδου ἀποκτενοῦντες Νικίαν τὸν Ἡροσκαμάνδρου
greek_tokens: anisthmi ek o sumposion o timarxos kai filhmwn o filhmonidou apokteinw nikias o hroskamandrou epistamai men autos monos o epiboulh o de timarxos anisthmi pros egw eipon tis legw fhmi eimi swkraths su men pinw egw de deomai poi ecanisthmi hkw de oligos usteron ean tuxos kai egw gignomai o fwnh kai eipon pros autos mhdamos fhmi anisthmi gignomai gar egw o eqw shmeion o daimonion kai os epwxato
english_head: son of Philemonides, got up from the wine-party to kill Nicias, son of Heroscamandrus, those two alone had knowledge of the plot; and Timarchus, as he got up, said to me: What say you, Socrates? Go on drinking, all of you; I have to get up and go somewhere, but I will join you a little later, if I get the chance. Then occurred that voice of mine, and I said to him: No, no, do not get up; for my accustomed spiritual sign has occurred to me.

greek_head: καὶ διαλιπὼν χρόνον αὖθις ὡρμᾶτο ἰέναι καὶ ἔφη Εἶμι δή Σώκρατες αὖθις ἐγένετο ἡ φωνή αὖθις οὖν αὐτὸν
greek_tokens: kai dialeipw xronos auqis ormaw eimi kai fhmi eimi dh swkraths auqis gignomai o fwnh auqis oun autos anagkazw epwxato o tritos boulomai egw lanqanw anisthmi ouketi eipon egw oudeis alla lanqanw epithrew allote o new exw kai outws oixomai apeimi kai diaprassw ek os eimi apoqnhskw oqen dh outos eipon pros o adelfos osper nun su egw ostis eimi apoqnhskw dia o egw apistew eti toinun peri o en sikelia
english_head: So he stopped. Then after an interval of time he again started to go, and said: Well, I am going, Socrates. Again the voice occurred, and so again I constrained him to stop. The third time, wishing to give me the slip, he got up without saying another word to me; he gave me the slip by watching until my attention was turned elsewhere. Thus it was that he went right off and committed the deed which was the cause of his going then to his death. And hence it was that he spoke those words to his brother which I quoted to you just now, that he was going to his death because he had not taken my advice.

greek_head: πολλῶν ἀκούσεσθον ἃ ἐγὼ ἔλεγον περὶ τῆς διαφθορᾶς τοῦ στρατοπέδου καὶ τὰ μὲν παρεληλυθότα τῶν εἰδότων ἔστιν
greek_tokens: polus akouw o egw elegos peri o diafqora o stratopedon kai o men parerxomai o oida eimi akew peirw de ecesti nuni lambanw o shmeion ei ara tis legw epi gar o epi strateia ecormaw sanniwnos o kalew gignomai egw o shmeion oixomai de nun meta qrasullos strateuw euqus efesos kai iwnios egw oun oiomai ekeinos h apoqnhskw h omos tis outos ge elaunw kai peri ge o stratia o allos panu fobew
english_head: And moreover, in regard to the Sicilian business, many will tell you what I said about the destruction of the army. As to bygones, you may hear from those who know: but there is an opportunity now of testing the worth of what the sign says. For as the handsome Sannio was setting out on campaign, the sign occurred to me, and he has gone now with Thrasyllus on an expedition bound for Ephesus and Ionia. I accordingly expect him to be either killed or brought very near it, and I have great fears for our force as a whole.

greek_head: Ταῦτα δὴ πάντα εἴρηκά σοι ὅτι ἡ δύναμις αὕτη τοῦ δαιμονίου τούτου καὶ εἰς τὰς συνουσίας τῶν μετ' ἐμοῦ συνδιατριβόντων
greek_tokens: outos dh pas erw su ostis o dunamis outos o daimonion outos kai eis o sunousia o meta egw sundiatribw o apas dunamai polus men gar enantioomai kai ou eimi outos wfelew meta egw diatribw wste ou oios su egw outos sundiatribw polus de suneimi men ou diakwluw wfelew de oudeis suneimi os de an sullambanw o sunousia o o daimonion dunamis outos eimi os kai su aisqanomai taxus gar paraxrhma epididwmi kai outos au o epididwmi
english_head: Now I have told you all this, because this spiritual power that attends me also exerts itself to the full in my intercourse with those who spend their time with me. To many, indeed, it is adverse, and it is not possible for these to get any good by conversing with me, and I am therefore unable to spend my time in conversing with them. And there are many with whom it does not prohibit my intercourse, yet the intercourse does them no good. But those who are assisted in their intercourse by that spiritual power are the persons whom you have noticed; for they make rapid progress there and then. And of these, again, who make progress some find the benefit

greek_head: οἱ μὲν καὶ βέβαιον ἔχουσι καὶ παραμόνιμον τὴν ὠφελίαν πολλοὶ δέ ὅσον ἂν μετ' ἐμοῦ χρόνον ὦσιν θαυμάσιον
greek_tokens: e men kai bebaios exw kai paramonimos o wfeleia polus de osos an meta egw xronos eimi qaumasios epididwmi epeidan de egw apexw palin oudeis diaferw ostis outos pote pasxw aristeidhs o lusimaxos us o aristeidhs diatribw gar meta egw pampolus epididwmi en oligos xronos epeita autos strateia tis gignomai kai oixomai ekpleos hkw de katalambanw meta egw diatribw qoukudidhs o melhsihs uios o qoukudidhs
english_head: both solid and enduring; while there are many who, for as long a time as they are with me, make wonderful progress, but when they are parted from me relapse, and are no different from anybody else. This once befell Aristeides, son of Lysimachus, son of Aristeides. For by conversing with me he had made immense progress in a little time; and then he had to go on an expedition, and he went and sailed away. On his return he found that Thucydides, son of Melesias, son of Thucydides, had been conversing with me. Now Thucydides, the day before, had quarrelled with me

greek_head: ὁ δὲ Θουκυδίδης τῇ προτεραίᾳ μοι δι' ἀπεχθείας ἐν λόγοις τισὶν ἐγεγόνει ἰδὼν οὖν με ὁ Ἀριστείδης ἐπειδὴ
greek_tokens: o de qoukudidhs o proteraios egw dia apexqeia en logos tis gignomai eidon oun egw o aristeidhs epei aspazomai te kai allos dialegw qoukudidhs de fhmi akouw eimi swkraths semnunw atta pros su kai xalepainw ws tis eimi eimi gar fhmi egw outws tis de ou oida fhmi prin sos suggignomai oios eimi o andrapodon ou eoika ge fhmi egw nh o qeos alla meis kai autos ge fhmi katagelastos
english_head: over some arguments we had had. So when Aristeides saw me, after greeting me and talking of other affairs, he said: But Thucydides, I hear, Socrates, is somewhat on his dignity with you, and is annoyed as though he were somebody. Yes, that is so, I replied. Well, but does he not know, he said, what a sad slave he was, before he associated with you? It seems not, I replied, upon my soul. But indeed I myself also, he said, am in a ridiculous position,

greek_head: ἔχω ὦ Σώκρατες Τί μάλιστα ἔφην ἐγώ Ὅτι ἔφη πρὶν μὲν ἐκπλεῖν ὁτῳοῦν ἀνθρώπῳ οἷός τ' ἦ διαλέγεσθαι
greek_tokens: exw eimi swkraths tis malista fhmi egw ostis fhmi prin men ekplew ostis anqrwpos oios su eimi dialegw kai mhdeis xeirwn fainw en o logos wste kai diwkw o sunousia o xarieis anqrwpos nuni de enantion feugw an tis kai aisqanomai paideuw outws aisxunw epi o emautou fauloths poteros de eimi de egw ecaifnhs su proleipw outos o dunamis h kata mikros kata mikros eimi de os
english_head: Socrates. How exactly? I asked. Because, he replied, before I sailed away, I was able to discuss things with anybody, and show myself inferior to none in argument, so that I even sought out the debates of the most accomplished people: but now, on the contrary, I shun them, wherever I notice there is anyone of education, so ashamed I am of my own ineptitude. Tell me, I said, did this power forsake you of a sudden, or little by little? Little by little, he replied. And when it was present with you, I asked,

greek_head: Ἡνίκα δέ σοι παρεγένετο ἦν δ' ἐγώ πότερον μαθόντι παρ' ἐμοῦ τι παρεγένετο ἤ τινι ἄλλῳ τρόπῳ Ἐγώ σοι
greek_tokens: hnika de su paragignomai eimi de egw poteros manqanw para egw tis paragignomai h tis allos tropos egw su eraomai fhmi eimi swkraths apistos men nh o qeos alhqhs de egw gar manqanw men para su oudeis pwpote ws autos oida epididwmi de opote su suneimi kan ei en o autos monos oikia eimi mh en o autos de oikhma mallon de opote en o autos oikhma kai egw dokew polus mallon opote en o autos oikhma eimi legw su blepw pros
english_head: was it present through your having learnt something from me, or in some other way? I will tell you, Socrates, he said, what is incredible, upon my soul, yet true. For I never yet learnt anything from you, as you know yourself: but I made progress, whenever I was with you, if I was merely in the same house, without being in the same room, but more progress, when I was in the same room. And it seemed to me to be much more when I was in the same room and looked at you as you were speaking, than when

greek_head: σέ μᾶλλον ἢ ὁπότε ἄλλοσε ὁρῴην πολὺ δὲ μάλιστα καὶ πλεῖστον ἐπεδίδουν ὁπότε παρ' αὐτόν σε καθοίμην ἐχόμενός
greek_tokens: sos mallon h opote allote oraw polus de malista kai pleistos epididwmi opote para autos su kaqhmai exw su kai aptw nun de eimi de os pas ekeinos o ecis ekrew eimi oun eimi qeages toioutos o hmeteros sunousia ean men o qeaw filos eimi panu polus epididwmi kai taxus ei de mh ou oraw oun mh su asfalhs eimi para ekeinos tis paideuw e egkratew autos eimi o wfeleia os wfelew o anqrwpos mallon h para egw ostis an tuxh outos prassw
english_head: I turned my eyes elsewhere: but my progress was far the greatest and most marked whenever I sat beside you and held and touched you. Now, however, he said, that condition has all oozed away. Such then, Theages, is the intercourse you would have with me: if God so wills, you will make very great and rapid progress, but otherwise, you will not. Consider, therefore, if it is not safer for you to be educated by one of those persons who have command themselves of the benefit which they bestow on mankind, rather than follow the course on which you may chance with me.

greek_head: Ἐμοὶ μὲν τοίνυν δοκεῖ ὦ Σώκρατες ἡμᾶς οὑτωσὶ ποιῆσαι ἀποπειραθῆναι τοῦ δαιμονίου τούτου συνόντας
greek_tokens: egw men toinun dokew eimi swkraths egw outwsi poiew apopeiraomai o daimonion outos suneimi allhlwn kai ean men pareikw egw outos beltistos ei de mh tote hdh paraxrhma bouleuw ostis draw eite allos suneimi eite kai autos o qew o sos gignomai peiraw paramuqeomai euxh te kai qusia kai allos ostis an e mantis echgeomai mhketi pros outos anteipon eimi swkraths o meirakion eu gar legw qeaghs alla ei dokew xrh outws poiew outws poiew
english_head: Well then, I decide, Socrates, that our plan shall be to make trial of that spiritual sign by associating with each other. Thus, if it leaves us free, that will be best of all; if it does not, it will be time then for us to consider, at the moment, what we shall do—whether we shall associate with someone else, or try to conciliate the divine sign itself that occurs to you with prayers and sacrifices and anything else that the seers may indicate. In view of this, Socrates, say no more in opposition to the lad; for Theages is right in what he says. Well, if you consider that this is what we ought to do, let us do it.
